title: Introducing Netcompany Ineos: Danish IT company join British team on five-year deal
content:
Cyclist
Introducing Netcompany Ineos: Danish IT company join British team on five-year deal

After seven years as the sole title sponsor of the its pro cycling team, Ineos will morph into Netcompany Ineos from the Giro d’Italia after the announcement of a new five-year partnership.

The team’s new identity was unveiled this morning in London, with the team emphasising that the deal ‘will provide security, stability and continuity’. What it also involves is a lot of AI.

While the new sponsor is Danish, the team will continue to ride under a British flag once the Netcompany Ineos name gets its racing debut next month at the Giro, which starts on 8th May in Bulgaria. This includes a visual makeover, with the team’s kit will change to primarily a greeny grey colour.

Cyclist was at the launch in London, hearing about the partnership, Netcompany’s AI plans for the team and its ambitions.

What does it mean for the team?

Starting from the Giro d’Italia, the British team will ride under the Netcompany Ineos name. The jersey will change from then, but team staff have hinted that the light bib shorts unveiled at the beginning of the year could still make a comeback later in the season.

Initial reports suggested that Netcompany’s deal would be in the region of €100m over five years, translating to €20m per year. Ineos Grenadiers 2025 budget was rumoured to be in the region of €45m to €50m. This is still some €10m off the reported budget of UAE Team Emirates-XRG.

While that final figure hasn’t been publicly revealed, the team has stated its ambition to win the Tour de France for an eighth time within the next five years.

What is Netcompany?

Jim Ratcliffe’s Ineos will now share sponsor space with Danish firm Netcompany.

Founded in 1999, Netcompany is an IT firm, mainly involved in software and AI solutions. The company is headquartered in Copenhagen and employs 10,000 people across 12 countries. Last year, the company reported yearly revenue of over £900m and a net profit of around £150m.

Its work is centred on emerging IT markets and technology, notably the usage of AI. A big part of the partnership with Ineos will be the use of Netcompany’s Pulse AI software.

AI entering the Ineos ring

As part of the announcement in London this morning, the team were enthusiastic about Netcompany’s Pulse platform. The AI software compiles data and can be used to smooth logistics, as proven by its usage by international airports and transport systems.

During the presentation, it was mentioned that the software could be used to influence the team’s logistics, training and even tactics. Netcompany believes the team has an early jump on its competition through this platform.

‘We have our own platform. It’s our asset,’ Netcompany CEO André Rogaczewski said. ‘We are ahead, but I think the whole sport will move towards AI. We’ve got five years to become better at what we do using technology and data, but I think every cycling team should do this with AI.’

Win the Tour de France in five years

Rogaczewski was ambitious about the team’s plans to win the Tour de France within five years.

‘We’re going to have a mean winning machine,’ he said. ‘We have a huge ambition to win the Tour de France, and we want to win it intelligently.’

Finding the rider to accomplish this, however, is another challenge. Ineos may have bagged Oscar Onley in the 11th hour, but the squad missed out on Remco Evenepoel’s signature last year, despite a concerted effort. This is one of several big losses in recent transfer windows. Sir Dave Brailsford holds some theories behind this ambition, instead backing youth over current star names.

‘Clearly there’s one guy who’s pretty good at the minute, and there’s another very young guy who’s pretty good,’ Brailsford said, alluding to Tadej Pogačar and Paul Seixas. ‘Everybody could just say “game over” but I don’t believe that. I think in any sport, you need somebody to close the gap. You might not have somebody with the same genetics, but with the right training and the advantage of getting every last drop of performance out, you’re going to get close. I think it’s coming. I think there’ll soon be a crop of youngsters who will close that gap.’

‘The narrative around the team is pretty good at the minute,’ former rider and current Ineos director of racing Geraint Thomas told Cyclist. ‘It feels like it’s an evolution of the team – 3.0, so to speak. We’re at the start of a really exciting journey now, and I think people in other teams see that and hopefully want to be a part of that as well. That makes a big difference when it comes to signing riders. We’ve got that solid foundation now, so it’s all about the process going forward and targeting those big goals.’

Ratcliffe to stay, name to change

Netcompany will appear first in the team’s new branding, although the team is said to be a collaboration between the two. As for Jim Ratcliffe, the British tycoon will remain the team’s owner.

TotalEnergies will still to be involved in the team, although a title role now looks unlikely given the announcement of this new deal. Contrary to previous rumours, the team will keep its British licence.

‘We’re all here for the same purpose,’ Rogaczewski said. ‘The most important thing is that we have a stable environment around the team. I think that’s the most important thing, and that will help us to strategically look towards the next five years and become very successful.’

‘They deserve it. The UK is one of Europe’s biggest countries and it really deserves a winning team again. At least now we are paving the way for intelligent decision-making to build a modern cycling team.’

The post Introducing Netcompany Ineos: Danish IT company join British team on five-year deal appeared first on Cyclist.
author: Ewan Wilson | Staff Writer
